10.1 The Molecular Structure and Behavior of Lipids

Major Functions and Properties of Lipids
Major functions of lipids include: energy storage, membrane structure, and signaling
Unlike carbohydrates, amino acids, nucleotides, or lipids have limited solubility in aqueous media
Most lipids are amphipathic and they contain both hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions

Fatty Acids (1 of 3)
are major constituents of lipids
In a fatty acid, a hydrophilic carboxylate group is attached to one end of a hydrocarbon chain (containing typically between 12 and 24 carbons)
In their hydrocarbon chains, unsaturated fatty acids contain one or more cis C–C double bonds, whereas saturated fatty acids contain none
The fluidity of fatty acids decreases as the chain length increases and the number of cis double bonds decreases

Fatty Acids (2 of 3)
Representative structures

Fatty Acids (3 of 3)
Table 10.1 Some biologically important fatty acids

Fats (1 of 2)
In fat (or triacylglyceride), glycerol is esterified with three fatty acids
Fat can be used for metabolic energy storage because its carbon atoms are highly reduced
Fat can act as a source for energy or heat production, and for thermal insulation in organisms

Fats (2 of 2)
Structure of a simple triacylglycerol
Figure 10.2 The structure of tristearin, a simple fat.

are formed by the treatment of fatty acids with strong
bases such as
to form a sodium or
potassium salt
this process is called saponification
form micelles around oils, emulsifying the oil
Unlike soaps, synthetic detergents such as sodium dodecyl sulfate (S D S) are not precipitated by calcium or magnesium ions
sodium dodecyl sulfate

Waxes
Natural waxes are formed when a long-chain fatty acid is esterified to a long-chain alcohol
Because of the extremely small polar region, waxes are completely water insoluble
Figure 10.4 Structure of a typical wax.
Structure of a typical wax
10.2 The Lipid Constituents of Biological Membranes

Lipids, Micelles, Bilayers (1 of 2)
Lipids are major constituents of all biological membranes
Fatty acids tend to form spherical micelles, while lipids with one hydrophilic head group and two hydrophobic tails (hydrocarbon chains) promote the formation of a bilayer, the structure seen in biological membranes
The major classes of membrane-forming lipids are glycerophospholipids, glycoglycerolipids, sphingolipids, and glycerosphingolipids

Lipids, Micelles, Bilayers (2 of 2)
Figure 10.5 Phospholipids and membrane structure.

Glycerophospholipids (1 of 2)
are the major class of naturally occurring phospholipids, that is, lipids with phosphate containing head groups
Figure 10.6 Glycerophospholipid structure.

Glycerophospholipids (2 of 2)

head groups

aThese are the
groups in Figure 10.6. In addition to this
variation, there is also a great deal of variation in the hydrocarbon tails

Glycoglycerolipids
are a second class of membrane lipids
have a carbohydrate linked to their head group

Sphingolipids (1 of 2)
are a third class of membrane constituents, in which a fatty acid is linked to the amino alcohol sphingosine via an amide bond

Sphingolipids (2 of 2)
Structure of sphingomyelin

Glycosphingolipids
constitute a fourth class of membrane lipids
are sphingolipids with glycans attached to their head groups
Figure 10.7 Examples of glycosphingolipids.

Cholesterol (1 of 2)
are a fifth class of membrane lipids that is based on a tetracyclic hydrocarbon structure and thus is significantly different from the other four classes
is only weakly amphipathic because of its hydroxyl group
The bulky, rigid cholesterol structure disrupts regular fatty acid chain packing in membranes
is the precursor to all steroids

10.3 The Structure and Properties of Membranes and Membrane Proteins

Membrane Structure – The Fluid Mosaic Model (1 of 2)
Biological membranes consist of lipid bilayers, in which membrane proteins are embedded
Biological membranes can be considered as oriented two-dimensional liquids, in which lipids and proteins diffuse more or less freely within their plane (fluid mosaic model)
However, biological membranes also contain some defined structures or domains, such as protein complexes or lipid rafts

Membrane Structure – The Fluid Mosaic Model (2 of 2)
Note that the protein content of membranes is much higher than shown in this schematic
Figure 10.9 Structure of a typical cell membrane.

When cells marked with fluorescent-labeled membrane proteins are induced to fuse, the proteins gradually mix over time

Amino acid hydrophobicity plot for bacteriorhodopsin, showing that the membrane-spanning helical regions are highly hydrophobic. This pattern of side chain hydrophobicity is typical of membrane-spanning proteins

Post-Translational Modifications that Anchor Proteins to the Membrane

Insertion of Proteins into Membranes
The insertion of proteins into the membrane bilayer (tan) is a cotranslational process and requires the ribosome (brown) and the translocon (purple), a multisubunit complex that acts as a protein conducting channel. The translocon complex facilitates the insertion of hydrophobic regions of protein sequence into the bilayer
Figure 10.17 Cotranslational insertion and folding of transmembrane helices in an integral membrane protein.

Membrane Rafts (1 of 2)
are separate membrane domains
are dynamic structures rich in cholesterol, sphingolipids, and glycosylphosphatidylinositol
possible roles in cell signaling and sorting of proteins into organelles
sterol-to-lipid ratio may be critical in determining raft formation
Figure 10.19 Atomic force microscopy image of membrane domains in a model membrane.

Membrane Rafts (2 of 2)
Figure 10.18 Membrane rafts.

Adaptation to Hydrophobic Mismatch
Membrane thickness is influenced by the proteins in the membrane
Figure 10.20 Adaptation to hydrophobic mismatch in a membrane.
10.4 Transport across Membranes

Membrane Transport Processes (1 of 2)
There are three different modes of membrane transport processes:
Nonmediated transport
This mode of transport is slow
Diffusion across a membrane is more rapid for those solutes that are hydrophobic, and significantly slower for polar/charged solutes (membranes are effective barriers to the diffusion of polar/charged molecules)

Membrane Transport Processes (2 of 2)
Facilitated transport
Diffusion of certain solutes is accelerated by specific pores, carriers, or permeases
Active transport
Couples a thermodynamically favorable process (usually A T P hydrolysis) to achieve transport against a concentration gradient

Transport Processes in the Cell
Figure 10.21 Specific transport processes.

Transport by a Carrier: Valinomycin (1 of 2)
Ionophores are produced by bacteria to kill competing bacteria by destroying the electrochemical gradients that store free energy (several are useful antibiotics)
Valinomycin, an antibiotic produced by certain Streptomyces species, acts as a potassium ionophore
The exterior of the ionophore contains hydrophobic amino acids; the interior polar environment that is specific for
binding ions the size of

Figure 10.23 Valinomycin, an antibiotic that acts as an ion carrier.

Transport of Saccharides by the G L U T Family of Permeases
Figure 10.24 A model for permease function.
Permeases are membrane spanning proteins that recognize specific molecules for transport

Cotransport: Symport versus Antiport
Figure 10.25 Models for symports and antiports.
A symport transports two solutes across the membrane in the same direction, while an antiport transports two solutes across the membrane in the opposite direction

Pore-Facilitated Transport (1 of 2)
α-Hemolysin is a pore-forming ionophore
α-hemolysin protomer structure

Pore-Facilitated Transport (2 of 2)
Gramicidin A toxin forms an open helical structure
Gramicidin is a 15 residue polypeptide with alternating l- and d-amino acids, forming a structure that as a dimer acts as an ion-selective pore
Figure 10.27 Gramicidin A, an antibiotic that acts as an ion pore.

Water Channels: Aquaporins (1 of 2)
Aquaporins are water channels that increase water transport in some tissues: erythrocytes, salivary glands, kidney
Plasma osmolarity varies considerably as the erythrocyte moves through lungs, capillaries, and kidneys, so rapid transport of water is critical to prevent rupture of the erythrocyte membrane
Aquaporins facilitate rapid transport of water and thereby maintain osmotic balance within a cell while preserving critical ion gradients

Water Channels: Aquaporins (2 of 2)

A B C Transporters (1 of 2)
(or A T P-binding cassette transporters) are a large class of active transporters
bind small molecules at the cytoplasmic side or on the inside of the membrane and translocate these molecules A T P-dependently to the extracytoplasmic side or to the outside
involve transporters associated with multiple drug resistance as well as cystic fibrosis (cystic fibrosis transmembrane regulator, which is a chloride ion channel)

A B C Transporters (2 of 2)
Figure 10.35 Proposed mechanism of action for ABC transporters.
10.7 Cotransport Systems

The Sodium-Glucose Cotransport System (1 of 2)
In cotransport, the unfavorable movement of a substance through a membrane is coupled to the favorable transport of another substance
In the sodium-glucose cotransport system, the sodium gradient across the membrane provides the driving force for the unfavorable transport of glucose

The Sodium-Glucose Cotransport System (2 of 2)
Figure 10.36 A schematic model for the sodium–glucose cotransport (symport) system.

Other Cotransport Systems
Table 10.7 Some cotransport systems
10.8 : Direct Coupling of A T P Hydrolysis to Transport Action Potentials, and Neurotransmissions

Neurons
are cells responsible for conduction of electrical impulses over long distances without significant signal loss
have specialized thin cell projections called dendrites and axons that act as the “wires” of the nervous system
Figure 10.37 Structure of a typical mammalian motor neuron.

Resting and Action Potential
The resting potential of a nerve fiber is determined primarily by the permeability of the
membrane to
Membrane depolarization
caused by the influx of
via voltage-gated sodium channels generates the action potential

Transmission of the Action Potential
Figure 10.39 Transmission of the action potential.

Chapter 10 Summary (1 of 2)
Lipids are mostly hydrophobic
some are amphipathic, having hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions
A fatty acid consists of a hydrocarbon chain and a terminal carboxyl group
Fatty acids are present in fats (triacylglycerols) for energy storage and insulation, and in membranes as constituents of various types of lipids
Membranes are bilayer structures containing proteins and lipids in a fluid mosaic

Chapter 10 Summary (2 of 2)
Transport through membranes can be accomplished by three different modes:
nonmediated transport (diffusion)
facilitated transport (pores, permeases, or carriers)
active transport (A T P-driven or cotransport)
